content.text:
Runge-Kutta methods achieve high-order accuracy using only evaluations of $f$, 
without requiring derivatives. They sample $f$ at carefully chosen points 
within each step, combining these samples to match the Taylor series to 
high order.

content.title: $s$-Stage Runge-Kutta Method
content.content:
An $s$-stage Runge-Kutta method has the form:
$$
k_1 &= f(t_n + c_1 h, \, y_n + h(a_{11}k_1 + a_{12}k_2 + \cdots + a_{1s}k_s)) \\
k_2 &= f(t_n + c_2 h, \, y_n + h(a_{21}k_1 + a_{22}k_2 + \cdots + a_{2s}k_s)) \\
&\vdots \\
k_s &= f(t_n + c_s h, \, y_n + h(a_{s1}k_1 + a_{s2}k_2 + \cdots + a_{ss}k_s)) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + h(b_1 k_1 + b_2 k_2 + \cdots + b_s k_s)
$$
The method is determined by coefficients $(a_{ij})$, $(b_i)$, and $(c_i)$.

content.title: Explicit vs Implicit Runge-Kutta
content.content:
- **Explicit RK:** $a_{ij} = 0$ for $j \geq i$. Each $k_i$ depends 
only on previous $k_1, \ldots, k_{i-1}$, so stages compute sequentially.

- **Implicit RK:** Some $a_{ij} \neq 0$ for $j \geq i$. The $k_i$ 
form a coupled system requiring simultaneous solution.

- **Diagonally Implicit (DIRK):** $a_{ij} = 0$ for $j > i$. Each 
$k_i$ depends on itself and previous stages, requiring only scalar solves.

content.title: Butcher Tableau
content.content:
A Runge-Kutta method is compactly represented by its **Butcher tableau**:
\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
\mathbf{c} & A \\
\hline
& \mathbf{b}^T
\end{array}
= \quad
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
c_1 & a_{11} & a_{12} & \cdots & a_{1s} \\
c_2 & a_{21} & a_{22} & \cdots & a_{2s} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
c_s & a_{s1} & a_{s2} & \cdots & a_{ss} \\
\hline
& b_1 & b_2 & \cdots & b_s
\end{array}
\]
For consistency, $c_i = \sum_{j=1}^{s} a_{ij}$ (the row sums equal $c$).

content.title: Butcher Tableaus for Euler Methods
content.problem:
**Forward Euler:**
\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
0 & 0 \\
\hline
& 1
\end{array}
\]
One stage: $k_1 = f(t_n, y_n)$, $y_{n+1} = y_n + hk_1$.

**Backward Euler:**
\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
1 & 1 \\
\hline
& 1
\end{array}
\]
One implicit stage: $k_1 = f(t_n + h, y_n + hk_1)$, $y_{n+1} = y_n + hk_1$.

**Midpoint (RK2):**
\[
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/2 & 1/2 & 0 \\
\hline
& 0 & 1
\end{array}
\]
Two stages: $k_1 = f(t_n, y_n)$, $k_2 = f(t_n + h/2, y_n + (h/2)k_1)$, 
$y_{n+1} = y_n + hk_2$.

content.title: Family of Second-Order RK Methods
content.content:
The general two-stage explicit RK method:
$$
k_1 &= f(t_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= f(t_n + \alpha h, y_n + \alpha h k_1) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + h\left[\left(1 - \frac{1}{2\alpha}\right)k_1 + \frac{1}{2\alpha}k_2\right]
$$
is second-order for any $\alpha \neq 0$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Match coefficients in the Taylor expansion. Let $y_{n+1} = y_n + h(w_1 k_1 + w_2 k_2)$.

Expand $k_2$:
$$
k_2 &= f(t_n + \alpha h, y_n + \alpha h k_1) \\
&= f + \alpha h f_t + \alpha h k_1 f_y + \bigo{h^2} \\
&= f + \alpha h (f_t + f f_y) + \bigo{h^2} = f + \alpha h f' + \bigo{h^2}
$$

The method gives:
\[
y_{n+1} = y_n + h[w_1 f + w_2(f + \alpha h f' + \bigo{h^2})] = y_n + h(w_1 + w_2)f + h^2 w_2 \alpha f' + \bigo{h^3}
\]

The exact solution:
\[
y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + hf + \frac{h^2}{2}f' + \bigo{h^3}
\]

Matching:
$$
w_1 + w_2 &= 1 \quad \text{(order 1 condition)} \\
w_2 \alpha &= \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(order 2 condition)}
$$

Solution: $w_2 = \frac{1}{2\alpha}$, $w_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{2\alpha}$.

content.title: Special Cases of RK2
content.problem:
1. **Midpoint method** ($\alpha = 1/2$): $w_1 = 0$, $w_2 = 1$.
\[
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/2 & 1/2 & 0 \\
\hline
& 0 & 1
\end{array}
\]

2. **Heun's method** ($\alpha = 1$): $w_1 = 1/2$, $w_2 = 1/2$.
\[
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 1 & 0 \\
\hline
& 1/2 & 1/2
\end{array}
\]

3. **Ralston's method** ($\alpha = 2/3$): Minimizes error bound.
\[
\begin{array}{c|cc}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
2/3 & 2/3 & 0 \\
\hline
& 1/4 & 3/4
\end{array}
\]

content.text:
The classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta method is the workhorse of 
numerical ODE solving.

content.title: Classical RK4
content.content:
The classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta method:
$$
k_1 &= f(t_n, y_n) \\
k_2 &= f(t_n + h/2, y_n + (h/2)k_1) \\
k_3 &= f(t_n + h/2, y_n + (h/2)k_2) \\
k_4 &= f(t_n + h, y_n + hk_3) \\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)
$$

The Butcher tableau for classical RK4:
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/2 & 1/2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/2 & 0 & 1/2 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
\hline
& 1/6 & 1/3 & 1/3 & 1/6
\end{array}
\]
The weights $(\frac{1}{6}, \frac{2}{6}, \frac{2}{6}, \frac{1}{6})$ are 
Simpson's rule weights, suggesting a connection to quadrature.

content.title: RK4 Error
content.content:
The classical RK4 method has:

- Local truncation error: $\bigo{h^5}$

- Global error: $\bigo{h^4}$

- 4 function evaluations per step
content.label: Theorem

content.title: RK4 Step-by-Step
content.problem: Solve $y' = t + y$, $y(0) = 1$ with one step of RK4 using $h = 0.2$.

content.text: Deriving high-order RK methods requires matching Taylor series coefficients.

content.title: Order Conditions for RK Methods
content.content:
An $s$-stage explicit RK method has order $p$ if and only if:

**Order 1:** $\sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i = 1$

**Order 2:** $\sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i c_i = \frac{1}{2}$

**Order 3:**
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i c_i^2 = \frac{1}{3}, \quad 
\sum_{i,j} b_i a_{ij} c_j = \frac{1}{6}
\]

**Order 4:**
\[
\sum b_i c_i^3 = \frac{1}{4}, \quad
\sum b_i c_i a_{ij} c_j = \frac{1}{8}, \quad
\sum b_i a_{ij} c_j^2 = \frac{1}{12}, \quad
\sum b_i a_{ij} a_{jk} c_k = \frac{1}{24}
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The number of order conditions grows rapidly:

| Order $p$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| Conditions | 1 | 2 | 4 | 8 | 17 | 37 |

This is why designing high-order RK methods is complex.

content.title: Butcher Barriers
content.content:
For explicit RK methods:

- Order $p \leq 4$: Need $s \geq p$ stages.

- Order $p = 5$: Need $s \geq 6$ stages.

- Order $p = 6$: Need $s \geq 7$ stages.

- Order $p = 7$: Need $s \geq 9$ stages.
These are the **Butcher barriers**—it becomes increasingly 
inefficient to achieve high order with explicit methods.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: 3/8-Rule RK4
content.content:
An alternative fourth-order method:
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/3 & 1/3 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
2/3 & -1/3 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 1 & -1 & 1 & 0 \\
\hline
& 1/8 & 3/8 & 3/8 & 1/8
\end{array}
\]
Also fourth-order, slightly more accurate than classical RK4 but less used.

content.title: Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg (RK45)
content.content:
The **Fehlberg method** uses 6 stages to produce both a fourth-order 
and fifth-order estimate, enabling adaptive step control:
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccc}
0 \\
1/4 & 1/4 \\
3/8 & 3/32 & 9/32 \\
12/13 & 1932/2197 & -7200/2197 & 7296/2197 \\
1 & 439/216 & -8 & 3680/513 & -845/4104 \\
1/2 & -8/27 & 2 & -3544/2565 & 1859/4104 & -11/40 \\
\hline
\text{4th} & 25/216 & 0 & 1408/2565 & 2197/4104 & -1/5 & 0 \\
\text{5th} & 16/135 & 0 & 6656/12825 & 28561/56430 & -9/50 & 2/55
\end{array}
\]
The difference between the two estimates gives an error estimate.

content.title: Embedded Runge-Kutta Pair
content.content:
An **embedded pair** of order $(p, p+1)$ computes two approximations 
$y_{n+1}^{(p)}$ and $y_{n+1}^{(p+1)}$ using the same $k_i$ values:
\[
y_{n+1}^{(p)} = y_n + h\sum_{i=1}^{s} b_i k_i, \qquad
y_{n+1}^{(p+1)} = y_n + h\sum_{i=1}^{s} \hat{b}_i k_i
\]
The difference estimates the error: $\text{err} \approx |y_{n+1}^{(p+1)} - y_{n+1}^{(p)}|$.

content.title: Adaptive RK with Embedded Pair
content.content:
algorithmic[1]
 Embedded pair of order $(p, p+1)$, $(t_0, y_0)$, initial $h$, tolerance $\epsilon$
 Solution at specified points with error $< \epsilon$
 $t \gets t_0$, $y \gets y_0$
$t < T$
 Compute all $k_i$ stages
 $y_p \gets y + h \sum b_i k_i$ Order $p$
 $y_{p+1} \gets y + h \sum \hat{b}_i k_i$ Order $p+1$
 $\text{err} \gets |y_{p+1} - y_p|$
 err $\leq \epsilon$
 Accept: $y \gets y_{p+1}$, $t \gets t + h$
 err $< \epsilon/10$
 $h \gets 2h$ Error small, increase step
 
 
 $h \gets h/2$ Error too large, reduce step
 

algorithmic

content.title: RK Method Comparison
content.problem:
Solve $y' = -y + \sin(t)$, $y(0) = 1$ on $[0, 5]$.

| Method | $h$ | Error at $t=5$ | $f$-evaluations |
| Euler | 0.1 | $2.3 \times 10^{-2}$ | 50 |
| RK2 | 0.1 | $1.8 \times 10^{-4}$ | 100 |
| RK4 | 0.1 | $3.2 \times 10^{-8}$ | 200 |
| RK4 | 0.2 | $5.1 \times 10^{-7}$ | 100 |
| DOPRI5 (adaptive) | varies | $< 10^{-8}$ | $\sim 80$ |

Key observations:

- RK4 with $h = 0.2$ beats RK2 with $h = 0.1$ in both accuracy and cost.

- Adaptive methods achieve target accuracy with minimal evaluations.
